claim: The zodiac section (entropy 3.7149 bits, lowest of all sections) uses a label-oriented encoding in which most word tokens are proper nouns or short nominal forms, producing a word-length distribution significantly shorter than the herbal section (mean word length < 4.5 vs herbal > 5.0) and a lower bigram entropy reflecting repetitive naming conventions
evidence_for: Zodiac entropy (3.7149) is the lowest of all sections â€” lower entropy implies more repetition or constrained vocabulary
Zodiac illustrations depict figures with labels, consistent with a naming/labeling register
Astronomical section also has low entropy (3.7471), suggesting illustrative sections share a constrained label vocabulary
evidence_against: Low entropy could reflect a different cipher key rather than a different register
Small sample (1,297 words, 12 folios) limits statistical power
Word-length difference between sections may be confounded by section-specific glyph frequency profiles
test: Compute mean and variance of word length (in glyphs) per section; run a two-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov test on word-length distributions between zodiac and herbal sections; also compute the fraction of zodiac words that appear in the top-200 most frequent words manuscript-wide
test_metric: Mean word length per section and KS test statistic for zodiac vs herbal word-length distributions; top-200 coverage fraction
test_threshold: KS p < 0.01 with zodiac mean < 4.5 and herbal mean > 5.0, and zodiac top-200 coverage > 0.70, would support a label register; overlapping distributions would suggest a cipher-level rather than register-level difference

tests_run.test: compute mean and variance of word length (in glyphs) per section; run a two-sample kolmogorov-smirno
tests_run.details: Observed avg word length: 5.03. Expected: ~5.0. Difference: 0.03.

reasoning: If zodiac is confirmed as a labeling register, its words are candidate proper nouns (star names, zodiac sign names) that could serve as cribs for decipherment via known medieval astronomical nomenclature
